title: ⚠ MAP KEY — these are MODERN political borders
description: All coloured regions on this synthesis map are MODERN country borders. None represent pre-colonial Indigenous sovereignties, pre-modern empires (Ottoman, Aztec, Mali, Tu'i Tonga), or colonial territories. The goal of this map is to visualise the SEVEN origin regions side-by-side so students can see the geographic pattern: every commodity we consume comes from a specific ecological zone with specific cultural stewards.

Origin 1 — Coffee (Ethiopia)
description: Coffee's ORIGIN — Kaffa highlands, Ethiopia. Modern border (post-1991). Still home to irreplaceable wild coffee genetic diversity.

title: Origin 2 — Tea (China)
description: Tea's ORIGIN — Yunnan Province, China. Modern border (post-1949). 1,000+ year monopoly before British Empire forced transplant to India.

title: Origin 3 — Chocolate (Ecuador)
description: Chocolate's ANCESTRAL origin — DNA evidence places earliest cacao in the upper Amazon basin of modern Ecuador 5,300+ years ago (Zarrillo 2018). Mesoamerican cultural origin (Aztec xocolatl, Maya Popol Vuh) follows later.

title: Origin 4 — Sugar (Papua New Guinea)
description: Sugar cane's ORIGIN — Saccharum officinarum was domesticated in New Guinea ~10,000 years ago. From here it spread via Austronesian navigation west through Asia, eventually reaching the Atlantic slave-trade system.

title: Origin 5 — Guayusa (Ecuador / Amazon)
description: Guayusa's ORIGIN — Amazonian cloud forest transition zone. Kichwa + Achuar cultivated for 1,500+ years as an obligate understory species. Cannot be grown without intact forest ecosystem.

title: Origin 6 — Kola (West Africa / Nigeria)
description: Kola's ORIGIN — West African rainforest belt. Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa, Akan cultural homelands. Fueled the medieval trans-Saharan trade network connecting Mali, Songhai, and North Africa.

title: Origin 7 — Kava (Vanuatu)
description: Kava's ORIGIN — Tanna Island, Vanuatu. Piper methysticum domesticated ~3,000 years ago by Ni-Vanuatu. Austronesian voyaging carried it east to Fiji, Samoa, Tonga, Hawai'i — one of the longest cultural plant migrations in human history.
